h1: Ohio Veteran Property Tax Exemption Appeal Letter Generator
metaTitle: Ohio Veteran Property Tax Exemption Demand Letter
metaDescription: Generate an Ohio veteran property tax exemption appeal demand letter. Challenge denied homestead exemptions for disabled veterans under R.C. 323.152.
statuteCitation: Ohio Revised Code § 323.151-323.157 (Homestead Exemption for Disabled Veterans)
deadline: Complaint must be filed with the County Board of Revision by March 31 of the following tax year
penalty: Full exemption restoration plus refund of overpaid taxes; up to $50,000 of market value exempted for qualifying disabled veterans
introParagraph: Ohio offers an enhanced homestead property tax exemption for honorably discharged veterans who are 100% service-connected disabled or rated totally disabled by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs. This exemption shields up to $50,000 of the home's market value from taxation—significantly more than the standard senior homestead exemption. Unfortunately, county auditors sometimes deny these applications due to incomplete VA documentation, missed deadlines, or misinterpretation of eligibility criteria. If you are a qualifying Ohio veteran whose exemption was denied or improperly reduced, you have the right to appeal. A well-drafted demand letter to the county auditor or Board of Revision is often the fastest way to resolve the issue without prolonged litigation, and it preserves your right to escalate the matter administratively or in court.
lawExplanation: Ohio's enhanced homestead exemption for disabled veterans is codified at Ohio Revised Code §§ 323.151 through 323.157. Under R.C. 323.152(A)(2)(b), a veteran who has been discharged or released from active duty under honorable conditions and who has received a total disability rating (100%) for a service-connected disability—or is rated totally disabled based on individual unemployability—qualifies for an exemption of up to $50,000 of the true value of their homestead. Unlike the standard senior or disabled homestead exemption, the disabled veteran exemption has no income limitation. The surviving spouse of a qualifying veteran may also continue receiving the exemption under R.C. 323.152(A)(2)(c), provided the spouse occupies the home and does not remarry. Applications are filed with the county auditor on Form DTE 105I, accompanied by a letter from the VA confirming the veteran's total disability rating. The auditor must approve or deny the application and notify the applicant in writing. If denied, R.C. 5715.19 governs the formal complaint process before the County Board of Revision. The Board reviews evidence, conducts hearings, and issues decisions that can be appealed further to the Ohio Board of Tax Appeals under R.C. 5717.01 or directly to the county Court of Common Pleas under R.C. 5717.05. Common reasons for denial include insufficient VA documentation, failure to demonstrate that the property is the veteran's primary residence, or auditor confusion between the disabled veteran exemption and the standard homestead exemption. Knowing which statutory subsection applies to your situation is critical when challenging a denial, because the eligibility criteria and exemption amounts differ substantially.
letterStrategy: A demand letter is often the most efficient first step in resolving a wrongful denial of the Ohio disabled veteran homestead exemption. The letter should be addressed to the county auditor who issued the denial, with a courtesy copy to the County Board of Revision. Begin by clearly identifying yourself, the property's parcel number, and the tax year at issue. Cite the controlling statute—Ohio Revised Code § 323.152(A)(2)(b)—and attach supporting documentation: your DD-214 showing honorable discharge, the VA disability rating letter showing 100% service-connected disability or individual unemployability, and proof that the property is your primary residence (driver's license, utility bills, voter registration). State plainly why the denial was incorrect, referencing the specific eligibility criteria you meet. Request reinstatement of the exemption, a refund or credit for any overpaid taxes, and written confirmation within 30 days. A demand letter accomplishes several goals: it creates a written record of your good-faith effort to resolve the dispute, it often prompts the auditor to reconsider without forcing a Board of Revision hearing, and it frames the legal and factual issues clearly should the matter escalate. Many auditors will correct administrative errors once the statutory basis is laid out. If the auditor refuses or fails to respond, your letter becomes evidence supporting your formal complaint to the Board of Revision.
proceduralNotes: Formal complaints challenging an exemption denial must be filed with the County Board of Revision by March 31 of the year following the tax year in dispute, per R.C. 5715.19. There is generally no filing fee for homestead exemption complaints at the Board of Revision level. Appeals from the Board of Revision to the Ohio Board of Tax Appeals must be filed within 30 days of the Board's decision under R.C. 5717.01. Ohio's small claims court limit is $6,000, but property tax exemption disputes are not heard in small claims—they follow the administrative appeal track. Veterans should retain copies of all correspondence and consider requesting a hearing transcript if the matter proceeds to the Board of Tax Appeals.

faq.q: Who qualifies for Ohio's disabled veteran homestead exemption?
faq.a: To qualify, you must be an honorably discharged veteran who has received a 100% service-connected disability rating from the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, or be rated totally disabled based on individual unemployability. You must own and occupy the home as your primary residence. Unlike the standard homestead exemption, there is no income limit. Surviving spouses of qualifying veterans may continue the exemption if they occupy the home and have not remarried. The exemption shields up to $50,000 of the home's true market value from property taxation.

faq.q: What is the deadline to appeal a denied veteran exemption in Ohio?
faq.a: Under Ohio Revised Code § 5715.19, you must file a formal complaint with the County Board of Revision by March 31 of the year following the tax year being disputed. For example, to challenge a denial affecting your 2024 taxes, the complaint must be filed by March 31, 2025. Sending a demand letter to the county auditor before this deadline can sometimes resolve the issue administratively. If the Board of Revision rules against you, you have 30 days to appeal to the Ohio Board of Tax Appeals.

faq.q: What documents should I include with my appeal?
faq.a: Include a copy of your DD-214 showing honorable discharge, your most recent VA disability rating letter confirming 100% service-connected disability or individual unemployability, proof of property ownership (deed or recent tax bill), and evidence that the home is your primary residence such as a driver's license, voter registration, or utility bills. If appealing as a surviving spouse, include the veteran's death certificate and your marriage certificate. Form DTE 105I is the official application; reference it in your appeal.

faq.q: Can I get a refund for prior years if my exemption was wrongly denied?
faq.a: Possibly. Ohio law allows for retroactive application of the homestead exemption in limited circumstances, particularly when the denial resulted from auditor error rather than applicant fault. R.C. 323.153 permits late applications under certain conditions. If you can demonstrate that you were eligible during prior tax years and the denial was improper, you may request a refund or credit for overpaid taxes. The county auditor or Board of Revision will determine the scope of any retroactive relief on a case-by-case basis.

faq.q: Do I need an attorney to appeal a veteran exemption denial?
faq.a: No, you are not required to have an attorney. Many veterans successfully handle Board of Revision complaints on their own, especially when the facts are straightforward and well-documented. However, if your case involves complex issues—such as disputed disability ratings, residency questions, or appeals to the Ohio Board of Tax Appeals—consulting with an attorney experienced in Ohio property tax law or a veterans service organization may be helpful. County Veterans Service Commissions also provide free assistance with benefits-related matters, including exemption appeals.
